message:
feat(repair): add background scrub daemon with backpressure-aware scheduling

Implement ScrubDaemon round-robin scheduler that continuously scans block
groups for corruption and auto-repairs using the existing ScrubWithRecovery
pipeline. Key additions:

- ScrubDaemonConfig with configurable intervals, backpressure thresholds,
  and cancellation polling granularity
- ScrubDaemonMetrics for live TUI observability (blocks scanned, corrupt
  found, recovered, scrub rate, backpressure yields)
- SystemPressure integration from asupersync for adaptive throttling
- Per-tick ScrubDaemonStep summaries with timing data
- RepairDetail evidence variant for ledger auditing
- New E2E test scripts: btrfs read-write smoke test and 5% corruption
  repair scenario
- Updated e2e README with test inventory

Also updates AGENTS.md with latest multi-agent conventions and syncs
beads issue tracker state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-15c","title":"EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)","description":"# EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)

## PURPOSE
Implement FrankenFS’s second key innovation: automatic corruption recovery via fountain-coded repair symbols.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
A meaningful portion of the foundation already exists:
- Codec core:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` implements deterministic group encode/decode via asupersync RaptorQ (SystematicEncoder + InactivationDecoder) with unit tests.
- Symbol layout:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed on-disk symbol header and group descriptor extension types.
- Scrub engine:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic block scrub pipeline that can surface corruption findings.

## WHAT’S STILL MISSING (THE REAL WORK)
1. Durable symbol storage I/O + reserved-space rules (so symbols exist after restart and are not allocated as user data).
2. Recovery orchestration (detect -> load symbols -> decode -> writeback -> verify) + evidence ledger.
3. End-to-end, reproducible corruption injection and recovery proof scripts.

## DESIGN REQUIREMENTS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Per AGENTS.md “alien artifact” bar for risky logic:
- Explicit invariants for recovery decisions.
- Evidence ledger (structured records) for every recovery attempt.
- No silent partial repair; fail loudly if redundancy insufficient.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC)
1. Repair symbols are generated and durably stored per block group (configurable overhead).
2. Controlled corruption triggers recovery attempts.
3. Successful recovery restores exact bytes and is verified.
4. Failures are graceful and explainable (clear error + evidence).
5. E2E script demonstrates bounded corruption + recovery deterministically with logs/artifacts.

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §4 (Self-Healing)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` (current storage model)

## Success Criteria

 {"id":"bd-15c.10","title":"Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols","description":"# Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols

## GOAL
Define and implement the protocol for when RaptorQ repair symbols are regenerated after data blocks change. Two strategies: eager (regenerate on every write) and lazy (regenerate on next scrub or background pass). The system must choose correctly based on workload and corruption risk.

## BACKGROUND
When a data block in a block group is modified, the existing repair symbols become stale — they encode the old data. If corruption occurs before symbols are refreshed, repair will restore the OLD data, not the new data. This is a correctness hazard.

## DESIGN

### Eager Refresh
- Regenerate all repair symbols for a block group whenever any block in the group is modified
- PRO: symbols always current, repair always correct
- CON: write amplification — every data write triggers O(overhead_ratio * group_size) symbol writes
- USE WHEN: low write rate, high corruption risk, metadata groups

### Lazy Refresh
- Mark block group as dirty when any block is modified
- Regenerate symbols during next background scrub pass or explicit refresh
- PRO: amortizes symbol regeneration cost across multiple writes
- CON: window of vulnerability — stale symbols during dirty period
- USE WHEN: high write rate, low corruption risk, data groups

### Hybrid Policy
- Default: lazy for data groups, eager for metadata groups (superblock, GDT, inode tables)
- Configurable: per-group override based on DurabilityAutopilot risk assessment
- If corruption posterior exceeds threshold (e.g., P > 0.01), switch group to eager
- Track dirty duration — if group stays dirty > timeout (e.g., 30s), force refresh

### Dirty Tracking
- Per-block-group dirty bitmap in memory
- Dirty timestamp for staleness tracking
- Integration with write path: set dirty bit on block write
- Integration with scrub daemon: clear dirty bit after refresh

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
enum RefreshPolicy {
    Eager,
    Lazy { max_staleness: Duration },
    Adaptive { risk_threshold: f64 },
}

struct GroupRefreshState {
    dirty: bool,
    dirty_since: Option<Instant>,
    policy: RefreshPolicy,
    last_refresh: Instant,
}
```

### Integration Points
- Write path (ffs-block or ffs-ext4/ffs-btrfs): set dirty bit after block write
- Scrub daemon: refresh stale groups during background pass
- DurabilityAutopilot: adjust per-group policy based on risk
- bd-820 (RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush): this bead generalizes that concept

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Eager refresh correctly regenerates symbols on every write to affected group
- [ ] Lazy refresh marks groups dirty and refreshes during scrub
- [ ] Hybrid policy applies eager to metadata, lazy to data by default
- [ ] Adaptive policy switches eager/lazy based on corruption posterior
- [ ] Dirty duration timeout forces refresh after configurable period
- [ ] Evidence records logged for every refresh (eager or lazy)
- [ ] Unit tests: eager refresh correctness after single write
- [ ] Unit tests: lazy refresh correctness after multiple writes then scrub
- [ ] Integration test: verify repair works correctly with both policies","notes":"## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, dirty, dirty_since_ms, last_refresh_ms on policy evaluation.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, max_staleness_ms, risk_threshold, posterior_mean when adaptive/eager/lazy decisions are computed.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, symbols_generated, generation_before, generation_after when a refresh is executed.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, dirty_age_ms, max_staleness_ms when stale lazy groups are force-refreshed.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, error on refresh failure or evidence-write failure.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderHare","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:12.974565074Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:21.052867096Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:12.974565074Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:05.419321319Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-820","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:37.150397412Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":78,"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section. -{"id":"bd-15c.11","title":"E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair","description":"# E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair

## GOAL
Prove that FrankenFS can survive 5% random block corruption with fully automatic repair, zero data loss, and a complete evidence ledger. This is THE acceptance test for the self-healing durability story.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Setup
1. Create a fresh FrankenFS image (e.g., 256MB ext4 with RaptorQ enabled)
2. Mount via FUSE, write a known dataset (1000 files with deterministic content)
3. Compute BLAKE3 checksums for all files
4. Unmount cleanly (symbols fully refreshed)

### Corruption Injection
5. Randomly corrupt 5% of all data blocks (flip random bits)
   - Use deterministic seed for reproducibility
   - Corruption must span multiple block groups
   - Must corrupt both data and some non-critical metadata blocks

### Repair
6. Remount the filesystem
7. Scrub daemon detects corruption and triggers repair
8. Wait for repair to complete (bounded timeout: 60s for 256MB)

### Verification
9. Read all 1000 files, verify BLAKE3 checksums match original
10. Zero files should be corrupted after repair
11. Evidence ledger must contain:
    - CorruptionDetected records for all corrupted blocks
    - RepairAttempted records for all repair operations
    - RepairSucceeded records for all successful repairs
    - ScrubCycleComplete summary

### Edge Cases to Test
- Corruption concentrated in one block group (stress-tests single group recovery)
- Corruption spread evenly across all groups
- Corruption of repair symbols themselves (secondary corruption)
- Corruption exceeding single-group repair capacity (graceful failure)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] 5% random block corruption: all files recovered, zero data loss
- [ ] Evidence ledger is complete and machine-parseable
- [ ] Test completes in < 120s for 256MB image
- [ ] Test is deterministic (same seed = same result)
- [ ] Test passes in CI (no hardware dependencies)
- [ ] Graceful failure when corruption exceeds repair capacity (> symbols available)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:27.944881986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:17.484881552Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","repair","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:27.944881986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:03.092814350Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:03.835132604Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:04.586717786Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":113,"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-15c.12","title":"E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness","description":"# E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness

## GOAL
Verify that the evidence ledger is complete, correct, and auditable after any repair operation. Every corruption event must have a corresponding detection record, every repair attempt must be logged, and the ledger must be self-consistent.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Single Block Corruption
1. Write known data, corrupt exactly 1 block, trigger scrub
2. Verify ledger contains exactly: 1 CorruptionDetected, 1 RepairAttempted, 1 RepairSucceeded
3. Verify all fields are populated (block_group, block_range, checksums, timing)

### Scenario 2: Multi-Group Corruption
1. Corrupt blocks across 5 different block groups
2. Verify ledger contains records for each group independently
3. Verify ScrubCycleComplete summary matches individual records

### Scenario 3: Repair Failure
1. Corrupt more blocks than repair symbols can handle in one group
2. Verify ledger contains RepairFailed with InsufficientRedundancy outcome
3. Verify no false RepairSucceeded records

### Scenario 4: Policy Decision Logging
1. Run multiple scrub cycles with varying corruption rates
2. Verify PolicyDecision records are emitted when autopilot adjusts overhead
3. Verify posterior parameters in records match expected Bayesian updates

### Ledger Self-Consistency Checks
- All timestamps are monotonically increasing
- Every RepairAttempted has a corresponding RepairSucceeded or RepairFailed
- Every CorruptionDetected has a corresponding RepairAttempted (unless deferred)
- ScrubCycleComplete.blocks_checked >= sum of individual corruption records
- No duplicate records (same block_group + block_range + timestamp)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 4 scenarios pass
- [ ] Ledger self-consistency checks pass for every scenario
- [ ] Records are valid JSONL (one JSON object per line)
- [ ] Records can be parsed by standard JSON tools (jq)
- [ ] Test is deterministic and CI-executable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:41.110041860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:20.907498983Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","evidence","repair","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:41.110041860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.11","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:25.776280150Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:02.350539577Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":135,"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-15c.2","title":"Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)","description":"# Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)

## GOAL
Persist RaptorQ repair symbols (and any required validation metadata) in a **durable, checksummed on-image format** so recovery can run after restart.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` already defines:
  - `RepairBlockHeader` (per repair block, CRC32C checked)
  - `RepairGroupDescExt` (OTI + repair region metadata, CRC32C checked)
  - constants and deterministic seed helpers
- There is **no** implemented I/O layer that:
  - reserves blocks for repair data
  - writes/reads symbol blocks
  - enforces atomic generation updates

## STORAGE STRATEGY (V1)
Use an **in-image reserved range at the end of each block group**, consistent with the layout described in `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs`.

Key requirement for ext4 images:
- Reserved repair blocks must be marked **allocated** in the ext4 block bitmap (and reflected in free-block counters), so the kernel allocator will never hand them out.

## SCOPE
1. Finalize the exact per-group layout:
   - how many blocks are reserved for:
     - validation digests (optional, but strongly recommended)
     - repair symbols
     - group metadata (`RepairGroupDescExt`) if stored in-band
   - which exact block(s) store `RepairGroupDescExt` and how it is located deterministically.
2. Implement the storage I/O layer (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - `read_group_desc_ext(group) -> RepairGroupDescExt`
   - `write_group_desc_ext(group, new_ext) -> ()` (atomic update rules)
   - `read_repair_symbols(group) -> Vec<(esi, bytes)>`
   - `write_repair_symbols(group, symbols, generation) -> ()`
3. Implement crash-safety for updates:
   - write all new symbol blocks first
   - only then advance the durable generation counter (so torn writes are detectable)
   - on open: choose the latest fully-valid generation
4. Integrate with ext4 metadata accounting (phase-gated but must be planned here):
   - ensure reserved blocks are excluded from allocation (`bd-huh.2`)
   - ensure group/superblock counters remain consistent

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit tests for serialization/parsing (already partially present):
   - roundtrip `RepairBlockHeader` and `RepairGroupDescExt`
   - checksum mismatch detection
2. Integration tests using an in-memory `BlockDevice`:
   - write a full group’s symbol set
   - read it back and verify byte-equality
   - simulate torn write (only half the blocks written) and assert the reader rejects the incomplete generation
3. Logging requirements:
   - tests must log group number, generation, and which block offsets were written/read on failure

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Repair symbol blocks can be written and read back deterministically.
2. Torn/partial updates are detected and do not silently “half-apply”.
3.  {"id":"bd-15c.3","title":"Implement corruption recovery pipeline","description":"# Implement corruption recovery pipeline

## GOAL
Wire together:
- corruption detection signals
- symbol retrieval (bd-15c.2)
- RaptorQ decode (`ffs-repair` codec)
- block repair writeback
- post-repair verification

…into an end-to-end, auditable recovery flow.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` provides deterministic `encode_group` / `decode_group`.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed symbol format.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic scrub engine that can surface corruption findings.

What’s missing is the actual **orchestration** and the integration point where reads/scrubs trigger recovery.

## SCOPE
1. Implement a recovery orchestrator (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - determine block group boundaries
   - identify corrupt block indices within the group
   - load repair symbols for the group
   - run `decode_group()` and obtain recovered blocks
   - write recovered bytes back to the device
   - re-validate repaired blocks
2. Define a minimal corruption signal model for V1:
   - Phase A (required for tests): explicit corrupt index list (test-driven)
   - Phase B (recommended): integrate with validation metadata (if bd-15c.2 stores digests) or format-specific validators
3. Evidence ledger (non-negotiable per AGENTS.md):
   - every recovery attempt must emit a structured record with:
     - group, generation
     - corrupt_count
     - symbols_available / symbols_used
     - decoder stats (`DecodeStats`)
     - outcome (recovered / partial / failed + reason)

## INTEGRATION POINTS (CHOOSE ONE; PREFER MINIMAL)
- Option A (preferred): a `RecoveringBlockDevice` wrapper in `ffs-block` that:
  - delegates to an inner `BlockDevice`
  - on read, runs a validator and triggers recovery on mismatch
- Option B: a CLI-driven repair path (`ffs scrub --repair` or similar) that:
  - scrubs blocks
  - repairs findings
  - re-scrubs to prove clean

The bead must pick one and make it testable.

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. End-to-end in-memory test:
   - create deterministic group blocks
   - encode + store repair symbols
   - corrupt N blocks (overwrite bytes)
   - run recovery
   - assert the original bytes are restored for all corrupted blocks
2. Failure-mode test:
   - corrupt more blocks than redundancy supports
   - assert recovery fails gracefully (no partial silent writes)
3. Evidence ledger test:
   - assert the ledger record contains all required fields and is parseable JSON

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Recovery succeeds when redundancy is sufficient and restores exact bytes.
2. Recovery fails loudly and safely when redundancy is insufficient.
3. Evidence ledger is produced for every attempt and is actionable.
4.  {"id":"bd-15c.4","title":"Add E2E corruption-injection + recovery verification scripts","description":"# Add E2E corruption-injection + recovery verification scripts

## GOAL
Add an end-to-end script that:
1. Produces (or copies) a working ext4 fixture image
2. Generates/stores repair symbols
3. Injects controlled corruption into a bounded set of blocks
4. Runs a read/scrub path that triggers recovery
5. Verifies the recovered bytes match the pre-corruption baseline
6. Captures an evidence ledger + detailed logs

## CONTEXT
Unit tests can validate encode/decode and parsing, but recovery is an end-to-end behavior across:
- block I/O
- checksum validation
- symbol store
- RaptorQ decode
- repair writeback

This bead makes recovery auditable and reproducible.

## DELIVERABLES
1. `scripts/e2e/ffs_repair_recovery_smoke.sh`
2. Uses `scripts/e2e/lib.sh` logging conventions.
3. Produces artifacts under `artifacts/e2e/<timestamp>/repair/`:
   - `before_checksums.txt`
   - `after_checksums.txt`
   - `corruption_plan.json`
   - `recovery_evidence.jsonl`

## CORRUPTION INJECTION RULES
- Inject corruption deterministically using a fixed seed.
- Corrupt a small percentage (for example 1-3%) of blocks in a single group.
- Record exactly which blocks were modified and how.
- Corruption method must be reversible/controlled (bit flips, zeroing, truncation is not acceptable unless explicitly tested).

## VERIFICATION
- Before corruption, compute `sha256sum` of a bounded set of files read through FUSE (or read via CLI read-path if available).
- After recovery, recompute and verify identical.
- Additionally verify block-level checksums where available.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Script is deterministic and idempotent.
2. Logs include enough detail to reproduce a failure.
3. Evidence ledger exists and is easy to parse (JSONL).
4. Script skips gracefully (exit 0) if repair pipeline is not available yet, but prints a clear reason.

## NOTES

-{"id":"bd-15c.5","title":"Implement background scrub daemon with Region-based scheduling","description":"# Implement background scrub daemon with Region-based scheduling

## GOAL
Launch an asupersync Region that performs continuous background scrub of all block groups, detecting corruption and triggering automatic RaptorQ recovery.

## BACKGROUND
From README: "Corruption detected by checksums triggers automatic recovery. No separate fsck pass. No downtime."

The existing pieces:
- `ffs-repair::scrub::scrub_group()` - scans one group ✅
- `ffs-repair::recovery::GroupRecoveryOrchestrator` - recovers one group ✅
- `ffs-repair::storage::RepairGroupStorage` - reads/writes symbols ✅

What's missing: the daemon loop that:
1. Iterates groups in round-robin
2. Calls scrub_group() on each
3. On corruption: triggers GroupRecoveryOrchestrator
4. Respects backpressure (yield under I/O pressure)
5. Produces structured evidence log

## DESIGN
```rust
pub struct ScrubDaemon {
    region: asupersync::Region,
    interval: Duration,
    state: Arc<ScrubState>, // tracks last-scanned group, stats
    metrics: ScrubMetrics,
}

impl ScrubDaemon {
    pub fn spawn(cx: &Cx, engine: Arc<FrankenFsEngine>) -> Self {
        // Launch Region that:
        // 1. Iterates groups 0..N
        // 2. scrub_group() each
        // 3. On corruption: attempt_recovery()
        // 4. Sleep(interval) between rounds
        // 5. Yield if cx.is_cancelled() or backpressure
    }
}
```

## DELIVERABLES
1. `ScrubDaemon` struct in ffs-repair or ffs-core
2. Region-based async scheduling via asupersync
3. Automatic recovery trigger on corruption detection
4. Structured JSON evidence log for all scrub/recovery events
5. Configurable scan interval and backpressure sensitivity

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Daemon starts and scans at least one group
2. Unit: Daemon detects injected corruption and triggers recovery
3. Unit: Daemon respects cancellation (stops promptly)
4. Unit: Daemon yields under backpressure (doesn't block reads)
5. Integration: Evidence log captures all events
6. Integration: Full filesystem scrub completes without error
7. Stress: Scrub under concurrent I/O load

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Daemon start (info): total_groups, interval_secs, config
- Round start (debug): round_number, starting_group
- Group scan start (trace): group_id
- Group scan complete (trace): group_id, duration_ms, blocks_scanned, errors_found

- Recovery started (info): group_id, corrupt_block_count, available_symbols
- Recovery success (info): group_id, blocks_recovered, duration_ms
- Recovery failure (error): group_id, reason, remaining_corrupt
- Backpressure yield (debug): current_group, pressure_source, yield_duration_ms
- Round complete (info): round_number, duration_secs, groups_scanned, total_corrupt, total_recovered
- Daemon stop (info): reason, final_stats
- Evidence entry (JSON): structured event for each scrub/recovery action

## METRICS (for TUI dashboard)
- blocks_scanned_total: Counter
- blocks_corrupt_found: Counter
- blocks_recovered: Counter
- blocks_unrecoverable: Counter
- scrub_rounds_completed: Counter
- current_group: Gauge
- scrub_rate_blocks_per_sec: Gauge

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Background scrub runs continuously during mount
2. Corruption is detected and recovered automatically
3. No noticeable performance impact on foreground I/O (<5% throughput reduction)
4. Clean shutdown on unmount (in-flight group completes)
5.  {"id":"bd-15c.6","title":"Wire automatic corruption recovery into scrub detection pipeline","description":"# Wire automatic corruption recovery into scrub detection pipeline

## GOAL
When scrub_group() detects a checksum mismatch, automatically trigger GroupRecoveryOrchestrator to recover the corrupted block using RaptorQ repair symbols.

## BACKGROUND
Currently scrub detects corruption (returns mismatch list) but the caller has to manually invoke recovery. This bead connects the two so corruption → recovery is automatic.

## DELIVERABLES
1. `ScrubWithRecovery` struct that wraps scrub + recovery
2. On mismatch: load repair symbols, attempt decode, write back corrected block
3. On successful recovery: refresh repair symbols (re-encode group)
4. On failed recovery: log evidence, mark block as unrecoverable
5. Return RecoveryReport with per-block outcomes

## DESIGN
```rust
pub struct ScrubWithRecovery {
    scrubber: GroupScrubber,
    recovery: GroupRecoveryOrchestrator,
    evidence_log: EvidenceLog,
}

impl ScrubWithRecovery {
    pub async fn scrub_and_recover(
        &mut self, 
        group: GroupNumber, 
        cx: &Cx
    ) -> Result<RecoveryReport> {
        // 1. Scrub group
        let findings = self.scrubber.scrub_group(group, cx).await?;
        
        // 2. If corruption found, attempt recovery
        if !findings.corrupt_blocks.is_empty() {
            let recovery_result = self.recovery
                .recover_group(group, &findings.corrupt_blocks, cx)
                .await;
            
            // 3. Log evidence regardless of outcome
            self.evidence_log.record(RecoveryEvidence {
                timestamp: Utc::now(),
                group,
                corrupt_blocks: findings.corrupt_blocks.clone(),
                recovery_result: recovery_result.clone(),
            });
            
            // 4. If recovery succeeded, refresh symbols
            if recovery_result.is_ok() {
                self.refresh_repair_symbols(group, cx).await?;
            }
        }
        
        Ok(RecoveryReport { ... })
    }
}
```

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit: Single corrupted block → automatic recovery → verified correct
2. Unit: Multiple corrupted blocks in same group → all recovered
3. Unit: Too many corrupted blocks (beyond RaptorQ capacity) → graceful failure report
4. Unit: Recovery of stale block (written after symbol gen) → expected failure with clear error
5. Integration: Full group corruption and recovery cycle
6. Integration: Evidence log contains all required fields
7. Stress: 100 groups with random corruption patterns

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
### Scrub phase
- scrub_start (debug): group
- mismatch_found (warn): group, block, expected_csum, actual_csum, csum_type

### Recovery phase
- recovery_start (info): group, corrupt_count, available_symbols
- symbol_load (trace): group, symbol_count, load_time_ms
- decode_attempt (debug): group, input_symbols, corrupt_positions
- decode_success (info): group, blocks_recovered, decode_time_ms
- decode_failure (error): group, reason, missing_symbols_needed
- block_writeback (trace): block, csum_after
- post_verify (debug): group, verify_result

### Symbol refresh
- refresh_start (debug): group, trigger (recovery/write)
- refresh_complete (info): group, symbols_generated, duration_ms

### Evidence logging (JSON structured)
```json
{
  "event": "recovery_attempt",
  "timestamp": "2026-02-13T04:00:00Z",
  "group": 42,
  "corrupt_blocks": [1024, 1025],
  "repair_symbols_available": 164,
  "decode_success": true,
  "blocks_recovered": 2,
  "blocks_unrecoverable": 0,
  "post_verify_ok": true,
  "total_duration_ms": 250
}
```

### Error logging
- io_error (error): operation, block, errno
- symbol_storage_error (error): group, slot, error
- checksum_mismatch_after_recovery (error): block, expected, actual (critical bug indicator)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Zero manual intervention for recoverable corruption
2. Evidence ledger captures every decision
3. Unrecoverable corruption clearly reported (not silent)
4. Post-recovery verification catches any recovery bugs
5. -{"id":"bd-15c.7","title":"Add comprehensive unit tests for self-healing repair pipeline","description":"# Add comprehensive unit tests for self-healing repair pipeline

## GOAL
Exhaustive unit test coverage for the RaptorQ encode/decode, scrub, storage, and recovery pipeline.

## DELIVERABLES
1. Codec tests: encode group, corrupt N blocks, decode succeeds for N ≤ symbol count
2. Codec boundary tests: exactly at repair capacity, 1 over capacity → failure
3. Storage tests: dual-slot generation commit, torn-write detection
4. Scrub tests: checksum mismatch detection for CRC32C and BLAKE3
5. Recovery tests: full pipeline (detect → load symbols → decode → writeback → verify)
6. Evidence tests: all recovery decisions captured in JSON ledger

 {"id":"bd-15c.8","title":"Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)","description":"# Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)

## GOAL
Every repair action, corruption detection, policy decision, and scrub cycle must produce a durable, auditable evidence record persisted as append-only JSONL (JSON Lines).

## BACKGROUND
The alien artifact methodology requires an auditable evidence ledger for every decision the system makes. This is non-negotiable for FrankenFS's self-healing story. Without durable evidence, repairs are invisible black boxes — unacceptable for a filesystem.

## DESIGN

### Record Schema
Each JSONL line is a self-contained JSON object with:
- timestamp_ns: u64 — monotonic nanosecond timestamp
- event_type: enum { CorruptionDetected, RepairAttempted, RepairSucceeded, RepairFailed, ScrubCycleComplete, PolicyDecision, SymbolRefresh }
- block_group: u32 — affected block group number
- block_range: (u64, u64) — start/end block numbers
- corruption_details: { blocks_affected: u32, checksum_expected: [u8;32], checksum_found: [u8;32] }
- repair_details: { symbols_used: u32, symbols_available: u32, decode_time_us: u64, verify_pass: bool }
- policy_context: { corruption_posterior: f64, overhead_ratio: f64, risk_bound: f64 }
- outcome: enum { Success, InsufficientRedundancy, IoError, Timeout }

### Persistence Strategy
- Append-only JSONL file per filesystem instance
- File location: reserved metadata area or configurable path
- fsync after each critical record (repair success/failure)
- Batch fsync for scrub cycle records (performance)
- Rotation: new file per mount session, old files archived

### Integration Points
- ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs — scrub daemon emits ScrubCycleComplete records
- ffs-repair/src/codec.rs — repair pipeline emits RepairAttempted/Succeeded/Failed
- Bayesian autopilot — emits PolicyDecision records when adjusting overhead
- Mount/unmount — session boundary markers

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Every corruption detection produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every repair attempt (success or failure) produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every scrub cycle produces a summary JSONL record
- [ ] Records survive crash (fsync on critical events)
- [ ] ffs-cli evidence show can pretty-print the ledger
- [ ] Records are machine-parseable (valid JSON per line)
- [ ] Unit tests: serialize/deserialize round-trip for all event types","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"HazyCave","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:31.155123171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:50:33.672096188Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T17:50:33.671995168Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["evidence","jsonl","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.8","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:31.155123171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-15c.9","title":"Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss","description":"# Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss

## GOAL
Replace ad-hoc threshold selection for RaptorQ overhead ratio with principled Bayesian decision theory. The system automatically adjusts repair symbol overhead (3-10%) based on observed corruption rate, using a formal expected-loss minimization framework.

## BACKGROUND
Current state: fixed overhead ratio. This is suboptimal — low-corruption drives waste space on repair symbols, while high-corruption drives may have insufficient redundancy. The alien artifact methodology demands principled, auditable policy decisions.

## MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK

### Beta Posterior over Corruption Probability
- Prior: Beta(alpha_0, beta_0) — weakly informative, e.g., Beta(1, 100)
- Observation model: each scrub cycle observes k corrupted blocks out of n checked
- Posterior: Beta(alpha_0 + k, beta_0 + n - k)
- Posterior mean: (alpha_0 + k) / (alpha_0 + beta_0 + n)

### Chernoff-Style Unrecoverable Risk Bound
- P(unrecoverable | overhead_ratio) = P(corrupted_blocks > decodable_symbols)
- For RaptorQ with overhead_ratio r and block group size B:
  - decodable_symbols = floor(r * B)
  - P(unrecoverable) = P(Binomial(B, p_corruption) > decodable_symbols)
- Compute via Beta-Binomial tail probability

### Expected-Loss Policy
- E[loss(overhead_ratio)] = P(unrecoverable) * data_loss_cost + overhead_ratio * storage_cost
- Minimize over overhead_ratio in [0.03, 0.10]
- data_loss_cost: configurable, default 1e6 (in arbitrary units)
- storage_cost: configurable, default 1.0 per unit overhead

### Metadata Elevation
- Superblock and GDT block groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- Rationale: metadata loss is catastrophic (entire FS unrecoverable vs single file loss)

### Update Schedule
- Recompute optimal overhead after each scrub cycle
- Apply lazily: next symbol refresh uses new ratio
- Log PolicyDecision evidence record with full posterior parameters

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
struct DurabilityAutopilot {
    alpha: f64,
    beta: f64,
    data_loss_cost: f64,
    storage_cost: f64,
    min_overhead: f64,  // 0.03
    max_overhead: f64,  // 0.10
    metadata_multiplier: f64,  // 2.0
}
```

### Key Functions
- update_posterior(corrupted: u64, checked: u64) — Bayesian update
- optimal_overhead() -> f64 — minimize expected loss
- optimal_overhead_metadata() -> f64 — with elevation multiplier
- risk_bound(overhead: f64) -> f64 — P(unrecoverable)
- expected_loss(overhead: f64) -> f64 — full loss computation

### Location
- New module: ffs-repair/src/autopilot.rs
- Integration: scrub daemon calls update_posterior after each cycle
- Integration: symbol refresh reads optimal_overhead before encoding

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Beta posterior correctly updated after each scrub cycle
- [ ] Expected-loss minimization selects overhead in [3%, 10%] range
- [ ] Metadata groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- [ ] PolicyDecision evidence records logged for every adjustment
- [ ] Unit tests: known corruption rates produce expected overhead selections
- [ ] Unit tests: edge cases (zero corruption, 100% corruption, single block group)
- [ ] Property test: optimal_overhead monotonically increases with corruption rate","notes":"## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::policy: fields checked_blocks, corrupted_blocks, groups_total on scrub-cycle observation intake.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::policy: fields posterior_alpha, posterior_beta, posterior_mean after posterior updates.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, metadata_group, selected_overhead, symbol_count, risk_bound, expected_loss for each policy decision.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, selected_symbols, layout_capacity, effective_overhead when clamping to reserved repair-block capacity.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, error when policy evidence append fails.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderHare","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:53.168595969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T20:17:32.901024753Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T20:17:32.901001229Z","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive Bayesian overhead autopilot in ffs-repair with tests and full gate pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bayesian","policy","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:53.168595969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:01.576524911Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":79,"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-2jk.8","title":"Implement E2E test framework with fixture lifecycle and detailed logging","description":"# Implement E2E test framework with fixture lifecycle and detailed logging

## GOAL
Build a reusable E2E test framework that all E2E test suites (ext4 RW, btrfs RW, FUSE, corruption recovery, degradation) can use. This avoids duplicating test infrastructure across beads.

## BACKGROUND
Multiple beads require E2E tests:
- bd-huh.5: ext4 write path E2E
- bd-2ju: btrfs RW E2E
- bd-3en1: FUSE runtime E2E
- bd-15c.4: corruption recovery E2E
- bd-lqwc: degradation E2E
- bd-375.3: btrfs RO mount E2E

All need:
1. Fixture image creation (ext4/btrfs with known content)
2. Mount/unmount lifecycle management
3. File operation execution and verification
4. Detailed structured logging (JSON)
5. Cleanup guarantees (unmount even on test failure)
6. Artifact collection (logs, evidence, flamegraphs)

## DELIVERABLES
1. `E2eTestContext` struct in ffs-harness:
   - `new(name, image_type, options)` → creates temp image
   - `mount(&self) -> MountHandle` → mounts via FUSE
   - `verify_file(&self, path, expected_content)` → read-and-compare
   - `inject_corruption(&self, block, bytes)` → flip bits
   - `collect_artifacts(&self, dir)` → save logs + evidence
2. `MountHandle`:
   - RAII: unmounts on drop
   - `exec_in_mount(|path| { ... })` → run closures inside mount
3. Structured JSON logging (see LOGGING REQUIREMENTS below)
4. `scripts/run_e2e.sh`: Shell wrapper that runs all E2E suites with cleanup

## TESTS
1. Framework can create ext4 + btrfs fixture images
2. Mount + unmount lifecycle works without leaks
3. Corruption injection produces detectable corruption
4. Artifact collection captures all logs
5. Cleanup runs even on panic (catch_unwind)

-{"id":"bd-2ju","title":"Add comprehensive E2E test suite for btrfs RW path","description":"# Add comprehensive E2E test suite for btrfs RW path

## GOAL
Create E2E tests for btrfs write path similar to bd-huh.5 for ext4.

## BACKGROUND
The ext4 RW smoke test (bd-huh.5) validates:
- File create/write/read roundtrip
- mkdir/rmdir
- rename
- unlink
- Persistence after unmount/remount

btrfs needs equivalent coverage plus COW-specific validation.

## DELIVERABLES

### 1. Script: scripts/e2e/ffs_btrfs_rw_smoke.sh
```bash
#\!/usr/bin/env bash
# btrfs RW smoke test for FrankenFS
set -euo pipefail
source "$(dirname "$0")/lib.sh"

# Generate fresh btrfs image
mkfs.btrfs -f "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs"

# Mount RW
ffs mount --rw "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs" "$WORKDIR/mnt"

# Test operations...
```

### 2. Test Operations (all required)
1. **File operations**:
   - Create file with echo
   - Write content (small: 10 bytes, medium: 4KB, large: 1MB)
   - Read and verify md5sum
   - Overwrite and verify
   - Truncate (extend and shrink)
   - Append

2. **Directory operations**:
   - mkdir single level
   - mkdir -p nested
   - rmdir (empty)
   - rmdir non-empty fails with correct errno

3. **Name operations**:
   - rename within directory
   - rename across directories
   - rename over existing file (atomic replace)
   - unlink regular file
   - unlink symlink

4. **Link operations**:
   - Create symlink
   - Read symlink (readlink)
   - Create hardlink
   - Verify hardlink shares data

5. **COW-specific tests**:
   - Write same file 10 times -> check generation advances
   - Verify btrfs tree root changed after writes (via btrfs inspect)

6. **Persistence tests**:
   - Unmount cleanly
   - Remount read-only
   - Verify all data intact
   - Verify metadata (size, mode, mtime) preserved

### 3. Logging Specification (in test script)
```bash
# Environment
export RUST_LOG=ffs=trace,fuser=debug
export RUST_BACKTRACE=1

# Per-test logging function
log_test() {
    local name="$1"
    local status="$2"
    local duration="$3"
    echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] TEST: $name STATUS: $status DURATION: ${duration}ms" >> "$LOG_FILE"
}

# Before/after state capture
capture_state() {
    local label="$1"
    echo "=== State: $label ===" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    ls -laR "$WORKDIR/mnt" 2>/dev/null >> "$LOG_FILE" || true
    btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree "$WORKDIR/test.btrfs" 2>/dev/null | head -100 >> "$LOG_FILE" || true
}
```

### 4. Fixture Management
- Generate fresh 256MB btrfs image in temp dir
- Pre-populate with test files if needed
- Clean up on exit (trap ERR and EXIT)
- Log cleanup actions

### 5. CI Integration
- Skip with exit 0 if mkfs.btrfs not available
- Skip if /dev/fuse not available (CI environment)
- Produce JUnit XML report for CI parsing

## FAILURE HANDLING
- On any test failure: capture full state
- Include `dmesg | tail -50` for kernel messages
- Include `journalctl -n 50` if available
- Preserve temp directory for debugging (print path)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All test operations pass on clean btrfs image
2. All operations logged with timing and result
3. COW semantics verified (generation increments)
4. Persistence verified across unmount/remount
5. Clean exit code (0 success, 1 failure, 0 skip)
6. Script runs in under 60 seconds
7.  {"id":"bd-2lpe","title":"Implement zero-copy I/O paths where possible","description":"# Implement zero-copy I/O paths where possible

## GOAL
Minimize data copies on I/O paths, applying mechanical sympathy principles.

## BACKGROUND
FUSE fundamentally requires kernel/userspace copies. But we can minimize userspace copies:
1. Direct buffer handoff between cache and FUSE
2. Vectored I/O for multi-block operations
3. Page-aligned allocations for OS optimization

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. BlockBuf Refcounting
```rust
pub struct BlockBuf {
    data: Arc<AlignedVec>,  // Shared, aligned buffer
}

impl BlockBuf {
    pub fn as_slice(&self) -> &[u8];
    pub fn clone_ref(&self) -> BlockBuf;  // Arc::clone
    pub fn make_mut(&mut self) -> &mut [u8]; // COW if shared
}
```

### 2. Aligned Allocation
```rust
pub struct AlignedVec {
    ptr: NonNull<u8>,
    len: usize,
    cap: usize,
}

impl AlignedVec {
    pub fn new(size: usize, align: usize) -> Self;
    // Align to page size (4096) for direct I/O compatibility
}
```

### 3. Vectored I/O
```rust
pub trait VectoredBlockDevice: BlockDevice {
    fn read_vectored(
        &self,
        blocks: &[BlockNumber],
        bufs: &mut [BlockBuf],
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Result<()>;
    
    fn write_vectored(
        &self,
        blocks: &[BlockNumber],
        bufs: &[BlockBuf],
        cx: &Cx,
    ) -> Result<()>;
}
```

### 4. FUSE Buffer Handoff
- Use fuser reply buffer directly where possible
- Avoid intermediate Vec allocations
- Consider mmap for large reads (if FUSE supports)

## TESTS
1. Unit: BlockBuf refcounting semantics
2. Unit: Aligned allocation alignment
3. Integration: Vectored I/O correctness
4. Benchmark: Copy count measurement

## LOGGING
- Buffer allocation (trace): size, alignment
- Copy detected (trace): source, dest, size
- Vectored I/O (trace): block count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No unnecessary copies in hot paths
2. Memory usage not increased
3. Performance improvement measurable
4. Correctness preserved","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:55:26.339499830Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:32.615713959Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["core","optimization","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2lpe","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:00.318748579Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2lpe","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:29.832494790Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":206,"issue_id":"bd-2lpe","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-2oa","title":"ffs-error: Decide mount-validation error variants (UnsupportedFeature/UnsupportedBlockSize/etc)","description":"Context: mount validation can fail because of unsupported feature bits, block sizes, or incompatible on-disk geometry.

+## MCP Agent Mail — Multi-Agent Coordination
+
+A mail-like layer that lets coding agents coordinate asynchronously via MCP tools and resources. Provides identities, inbox/outbox, searchable threads, and advisory file reservations with human-auditable artifacts in Git.
+
+### Why It's Useful
+
+- **Prevents conflicts:** Explicit file reservations (leases) for files/globs
+- **Token-efficient:** Messages stored in per-project archive, not in context
+- **Quick reads:** `resource://inbox/...`, `resource://thread/...`
+
+### Same Repository Workflow
+
+1. **Register identity:**
+   ```
+   ensure_project(project_key=<abs-path>)
+   register_agent(project_key, program, model)
+   ```
+
+2. **Reserve files before editing:**
+   ```
+   file_reservation_paths(project_key, agent_name, ["src/**"], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)
+   ```
+
+3. **Communicate with threads:**
+   ```
+   send_message(..., thread_id="FEAT-123")
+   fetch_inbox(project_key, agent_name)
+   acknowledge_message(project_key, agent_name, message_id)
+   ```
+
+4. **Quick reads:**
+   ```
+   resource://inbox/{Agent}?project=<abs-path>&limit=20
+   resource://thread/{id}?project=<abs-path>&include_bodies=true
+   ```
+
+### Macros vs Granular Tools
+
+- **Prefer macros for speed:** `macro_start_session`, `macro_prepare_thread`, `macro_file_reservation_cycle`, `macro_contact_handshake`
+- **Use granular tools for control:** `register_agent`, `file_reservation_paths`, `send_message`, `fetch_inbox`, `acknowledge_message`
+
+### Common Pitfalls
+
+- `"from_agent not registered"`: Always `register_agent` in the correct `project_key` first
+- `"FILE_RESERVATION_CONFLICT"`: Adjust patterns, wait for expiry, or use non-exclusive reservation
+- **Auth errors:** If JWT+JWKS enabled, include bearer token with matching `kid`
+

+## Beads (br) — Dependency-Aware Issue Tracking
+
+Beads provides a lightweight, dependency-aware issue database and CLI (`br` - beads_rust) for selecting "ready work," setting priorities, and tracking status. It complements MCP Agent Mail's messaging and file reservations.
+
+**Important:** `br` is non-invasive—it NEVER runs git commands automatically. You must manually commit changes after `br sync --flush-only`.
+
+### Conventions
+
+- **Single source of truth:** Beads for task status/priority/dependencies; Agent Mail for conversation and audit
+- **Shared identifiers:** Use Beads issue ID (e.g., `br-123`) as Mail `thread_id` and prefix subjects with `[br-123]`
+- **Reservations:** When starting a task, call `file_reservation_paths()` with the issue ID in `reason`
+
+### Typical Agent Flow
+
+1. **Pick ready work (Beads):**
+   ```bash
+   br ready --json  # Choose highest priority, no blockers
+   ```
+
+2. **Reserve edit surface (Mail):**
+   ```
+   file_reservation_paths(project_key, agent_name, ["src/**"], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true, reason="br-123")
+   ```
+
+3. **Announce start (Mail):**
+   ```
+   send_message(..., thread_id="br-123", subject="[br-123] Start: <title>", ack_required=true)
+   ```
+
+4. **Work and update:** Reply in-thread with progress
+
+5. **Complete and release:**
+   ```bash
+   br close 123 --reason "Completed"
+   br sync --flush-only  # Export to JSONL (no git operations)
+   ```
+   ```
+   release_file_reservations(project_key, agent_name, paths=["src/**"])
+   ```
+   Final Mail reply: `[br-123] Completed` with summary
+
+### Mapping Cheat Sheet
+
+| Concept | Value |
+|---------|-------|
+| Mail `thread_id` | `br-###` |
+| Mail subject | `[br-###] ...` |
+| File reservation `reason` | `br-###` |
+| Commit messages | Include `br-###` for traceability |

+## bv — Graph-Aware Triage Engine
+
+bv is a graph-aware triage engine for Beads projects (`.beads/beads.jsonl`). It computes PageRank, betweenness, critical path, cycles, HITS, eigenvector, and k-core metrics deterministically.
+
+**Scope boundary:** bv handles *what to work on* (triage, priority, planning). For agent-to-agent coordination (messaging, work claiming, file reservations), use MCP Agent Mail.
+
+**CRITICAL: Use ONLY `--robot-*` flags. Bare `bv` launches an interactive TUI that blocks your session.**
+
+### The Workflow: Start With Triage
+
+**`bv --robot-triage` is your single entry point.** It returns:
+- `quick_ref`: at-a-glance counts + top 3 picks
+- `recommendations`: ranked actionable items with scores, reasons, unblock info
+- `quick_wins`: low-effort high-impact items
+- `blockers_to_clear`: items that unblock the most downstream work
+- `project_health`: status/type/priority distributions, graph metrics
+- `commands`: copy-paste shell commands for next steps
+
+```bash
+bv --robot-triage        # THE MEGA-COMMAND: start here
+bv --robot-next          # Minimal: just the single top pick + claim command
+```
+
+### Command Reference
+
+**Planning:**
+| Command | Returns |
+|---------|---------|
+| `--robot-plan` | Parallel execution tracks with `unblocks` lists |
+| `--robot-priority` | Priority misalignment detection with confidence |
+
+**Graph Analysis:**
+| Command | Returns |
+|---------|---------|
+| `--robot-insights` | Full metrics: PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, critical path, cycles, k-core, articulation points, slack |

+## UBS — Ultimate Bug Scanner
+
+**Golden Rule:** `ubs <changed-files>` before every commit. Exit 0 = safe. Exit >0 = fix & re-run.
+
+### Commands
+
+```bash
+ubs file.rs file2.rs                    # Specific files (< 1s) — USE THIS
+ubs $(git diff --name-only --cached)    # Staged files — before commit
+ubs --only=rust,toml src/               # Language filter (3-5x faster)
+ubs --ci --fail-on-warning .            # CI mode — before PR
+ubs .                                   # Whole project (ignores target/, Cargo.lock)
+```
+
+### Output Format
+
+```
+  Category (N errors)
+    file.rs:42:5 - Issue description
+    Suggested fix
+Exit code: 1
+```
+
+Parse: `file:line:col` -> location | fix -> how to fix | Exit 0/1 -> pass/fail
+
+### Fix Workflow
+
+1. Read finding -> category + fix suggestion
+2. Navigate `file:line:col` -> view context
+3. Verify real issue (not false positive)
+4. Fix root cause (not symptom)
+5. Re-run `ubs <file>` -> exit 0
+6. Commit
+
+### Bug Severity
+
+- **Critical (always fix):** Memory safety, use-after-free, data races, SQL injection
+- **Important (production):** Unwrap panics, resource leaks, overflow checks
+- **Contextual (judgment):** TODO/FIXME, println! debugging
+

+## RCH — Remote Compilation Helper
+
+RCH offloads `cargo build`, `cargo test`, `cargo clippy`, and other compilation commands to a fleet of 8 remote Contabo VPS workers instead of building locally. This prevents compilation storms from overwhelming csd when many agents run simultaneously.
+
+**RCH is installed at `~/.local/bin/rch` and is hooked into Claude Code's PreToolUse automatically.** Most of the time you don't need to do anything if you are Claude Code — builds are intercepted and offloaded transparently.
+
+To manually offload a build:
+```bash
+rch exec -- cargo build --release
+rch exec -- cargo test
+rch exec -- cargo clippy
+```
+
+Quick commands:
+```bash
+rch doctor                    # Health check
+rch workers probe --all       # Test connectivity to all 8 workers
+rch status                    # Overview of current state
+rch queue                     # See active/waiting builds
+```
+
+If rch or its workers are unavailable, it fails open — builds run locally as normal.
+
+**Note for Codex/GPT-5.2:** Codex does not have the automatic PreToolUse hook, but you can (and should) still manually offload compute-intensive compilation commands using `rch exec -- <command>`. This avoids local resource contention when multiple agents are building simultaneously.

+## ast-grep vs ripgrep
+
+**Use `ast-grep` when structure matters.** It parses code and matches AST nodes, ignoring comments/strings, and can **safely rewrite** code.
+
+- Refactors/codemods: rename APIs, change import forms
+- Policy checks: enforce patterns across a repo
+- Editor/automation: LSP mode, `--json` output
+
+**Use `ripgrep` when text is enough.** Fastest way to grep literals/regex.
+
+- Recon: find strings, TODOs, log lines, config values
+- Pre-filter: narrow candidate files before ast-grep
+
+### Rule of Thumb
+
+- Need correctness or **applying changes** -> `ast-grep`
+- Need raw speed or **hunting text** -> `rg`
+- Often combine: `rg` to shortlist files, then `ast-grep` to match/modify
+
+### Rust Examples
+
+```bash
+# Find structured code (ignores comments)
+ast-grep run -l Rust -p 'fn $NAME($$$ARGS) -> $RET { $$$BODY }'
+
+# Find all unwrap() calls
+ast-grep run -l Rust -p '$EXPR.unwrap()'
+
+# Quick textual hunt
+rg -n 'println!' -t rust
+
+# Combine speed + precision
+rg -l -t rust 'unwrap\(' | xargs ast-grep run -l Rust -p '$X.unwrap()' --json
+```
+
+---
+
+## Morph Warp Grep — AI-Powered Code Search
+
+**Use `mcp__morph-mcp__warp_grep` for exploratory "how does X work?" questions.** An AI agent expands your query, greps the codebase, reads relevant files, and returns precise line ranges with full context.
+
+**Use `ripgrep` for targeted searches.** When you know exactly what you're looking for.
+
+**Use `ast-grep` for structural patterns.** When you need AST precision for matching/rewriting.
+
+### When to Use What
+
+| Scenario | Tool | Why |
+|----------|------|-----|
+| "How does the MVCC engine work?" | `warp_grep` | Exploratory; don't know where to start |
+| "Where is the repair workflow implemented?" | `warp_grep` | Need to understand architecture |
+| "Find all uses of `BlockDevice::read`" | `ripgrep` | Targeted literal search |
+| "Find files with `println!`" | `ripgrep` | Simple pattern |
+| "Replace all `unwrap()` with `expect()`" | `ast-grep` | Structural refactor |
+
+### warp_grep Usage
+
+```
+mcp__morph-mcp__warp_grep(
+  repoPath: "/dp/frankenfs",
+  query: "How does the MVCC snapshot isolation work?"
+)
+```
+
+Returns structured results with file paths, line ranges, and extracted code snippets.
+
+### Anti-Patterns
+
+- **Don't** use `warp_grep` to find a specific function name -> use `ripgrep`
+- **Don't** use `ripgrep` to understand "how does X work" -> wastes time with manual reads
+- **Don't** use `ripgrep` for codemods -> risks collateral edits
+

+## Beads Workflow Integration
+
+This project uses [beads_rust](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust) (`br`) for issue tracking. Issues are stored in `.beads/` and tracked in git.
+
+**Important:** `br` is non-invasive—it NEVER executes git commands. After `br sync --flush-only`, you must manually run `git add .beads/ && git commit`.
+
+### Essential Commands
+
+```bash
+# View issues (launches TUI - avoid in automated sessions)
+bv
+
+# CLI commands for agents (use these instead)
+br ready              # Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
+br list --status=open # All open issues
+br show <id>          # Full issue details with dependencies
+br create --title="..." --type=task --priority=2
+br update <id> --status=in_progress
+br close <id> --reason "Completed"
+br close <id1> <id2>  # Close multiple issues at once
+br sync --flush-only  # Export to JSONL (NO git operations)
+```
+
+### Workflow Pattern
+
+1. **Start**: Run `br ready` to find actionable work
+2. **Claim**: Use `br update <id> --status=in_progress`
+3. **Work**: Implement the task
+4. **Complete**: Use `br close <id>`
+5. **Sync**: Run `br sync --flush-only` then manually commit
+
+### Key Concepts
+
+- **Dependencies**: Issues can block other issues. `br ready` shows only unblocked work.
+- **Priority**: P0=critical, P1=high, P2=medium, P3=low, P4=backlog (use numbers, not words)
+- **Types**: task, bug, feature, epic, question, docs
+- **Blocking**: `br dep add <issue> <depends-on>` to add dependencies
+
+### Session Protocol
+
+**Before ending any session, run this checklist:**
+
+```bash
+git status              # Check what changed
+git add <files>         # Stage code changes
+br sync --flush-only    # Export beads to JSONL
+git add .beads/         # Stage beads changes
+git commit -m "..."     # Commit everything together
+git push                # Push to remote
+```
+
+### Best Practices
+
+- Check `br ready` at session start to find available work
+- Update status as you work (in_progress -> closed)
+- Create new issues with `br create` when you discover tasks
+- Use descriptive titles and set appropriate priority/type
+- Always `br sync --flush-only && git add .beads/` before ending session
+
+<!-- end-bv-agent-instructions -->
+
+## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
+
+**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below.
+
+**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
+
+1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
+2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
+3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
+4. **Sync beads** - `br sync --flush-only` to export to JSONL
+5. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
+

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+# ffs_btrfs_rw_smoke.sh - btrfs read-write E2E smoke test for FrankenFS
+#
+# This suite mirrors ext4 RW smoke coverage for btrfs and records
+# per-test timings plus a JUnit report for CI parsing.
+
+set -euo pipefail
+
+cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.."
+REPO_ROOT="$(pwd)"
+export REPO_ROOT
+
+source "$REPO_ROOT/scripts/e2e/lib.sh"
+
+export RUST_LOG="${RUST_LOG:-ffs=trace,fuser=debug}"
+export RUST_BACKTRACE="${RUST_BACKTRACE:-1}"
+export TMPDIR="${TMPDIR:-$REPO_ROOT/artifacts/tmp}"
+mkdir -p "$TMPDIR"
+
+e2e_init "ffs_btrfs_rw_smoke"
+e2e_print_env
+
+CURRENT_MOUNT_PID=""
+CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG=""
+CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT=""
+WORK_IMAGE=""
+MOUNT_RW=""
+MOUNT_RO=""
+
+JUNIT_FILE="$E2E_LOG_DIR/junit.xml"
+declare -a TEST_NAMES=()
+declare -a TEST_STATUSES=()
+declare -a TEST_DURATIONS_MS=()
+declare -a TEST_MESSAGES=()
+
+timestamp_ms() {
+    date +%s%3N
+}
+
+xml_escape() {
+    local raw="$1"
+    raw="${raw//&/&}"
+    raw="${raw//</<}"
+    raw="${raw//>/>}"
+    raw="${raw//\"/"}"
+    raw="${raw//\'/'}"
+    printf '%s' "$raw"
+}
+
+log_test() {
+    local name="$1"
+    local status="$2"
+    local duration_ms="$3"
+    e2e_log "[$(date -Iseconds)] TEST: $name STATUS: $status DURATION: ${duration_ms}ms"
+}
+
+record_test() {
+    local name="$1"
+    local status="$2"
+    local duration_ms="$3"
+    local message="${4:-}"
+
+    TEST_NAMES+=("$name")
+    TEST_STATUSES+=("$status")
+    TEST_DURATIONS_MS+=("$duration_ms")
+    TEST_MESSAGES+=("$message")
+
+    log_test "$name" "$status" "$duration_ms"
+    if [[ -n "$message" ]]; then
+        e2e_log "  detail: $message"
+    fi
+}
+
+emit_junit() {
+    local tests="${#TEST_NAMES[@]}"
+    local failures=0
+    local skipped=0
+    local i
+
+    for i in "${!TEST_STATUSES[@]}"; do
+        case "${TEST_STATUSES[$i]}" in
+            fail) failures=$((failures + 1)) ;;
+            skipped) skipped=$((skipped + 1)) ;;
+        esac
+    done
+
+    {
+        printf '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>\n'
+        printf '<testsuite name="ffs_btrfs_rw_smoke" tests="%d" failures="%d" skipped="%d">\n' "$tests" "$failures" "$skipped"
+        for i in "${!TEST_NAMES[@]}"; do
+            local name status duration_ms message duration_s
+            name="$(xml_escape "${TEST_NAMES[$i]}")"
+            status="${TEST_STATUSES[$i]}"
+            duration_ms="${TEST_DURATIONS_MS[$i]}"
+            message="$(xml_escape "${TEST_MESSAGES[$i]}")"
+            duration_s="$(awk "BEGIN { printf \"%.3f\", ${duration_ms}/1000.0 }")"
+            printf '  <testcase name="%s" time="%s">' "$name" "$duration_s"
+            if [[ "$status" == "fail" ]]; then
+                printf '<failure message="%s">%s</failure>' "$message" "$message"
+            elif [[ "$status" == "skipped" ]]; then
+                printf '<skipped message="%s"/>' "$message"
+            fi
+            printf '</testcase>\n'
+        done
+        printf '</testsuite>\n'
+    } >"$JUNIT_FILE"
+
+    e2e_log "JUnit report: $JUNIT_FILE"
+}
+
+capture_state() {
+    local label="$1"
+    e2e_log "=== State: $label ==="
+
+    if [[ -n "$CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT" ]] && [[ -d "$CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT" ]]; then
+        e2e_log "--- ls -laR $CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT ---"
+        e2e_run bash -lc "ls -laR '$CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT'" || true
+    else
+        e2e_log "Mount point not available for state capture."
+    fi
+
+    if [[ -n "$WORK_IMAGE" ]] && [[ -f "$WORK_IMAGE" ]] && command -v btrfs >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+        e2e_log "--- btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree (first 100 lines) ---"
+        e2e_run bash -lc "btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree '$WORK_IMAGE' 2>/dev/null | sed -n '1,100p'" || true
+    fi
+}
+
+capture_failure_diagnostics() {
+    e2e_log "=== Failure diagnostics ==="
+    if command -v dmesg >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+        e2e_log "--- dmesg (tail -50) ---"
+        e2e_run dmesg | tail -50 || true
+    fi
+    if command -v journalctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+        e2e_log "--- journalctl -n 50 ---"
+        e2e_run journalctl -n 50 || true
+    fi
+}
+
+skip_suite() {
+    local reason="$1"
+    record_test "suite" "skipped" 0 "$reason"
+    emit_junit
+    e2e_log ""
+    e2e_log "SKIPPED: $reason"
+    e2e_log "Log file: $E2E_LOG_FILE"
+    exit 0
+}
+
+fail_suite() {
+    local test_name="$1"
+    local duration_ms="$2"
+    local detail="$3"
+
+    record_test "$test_name" "fail" "$duration_ms" "$detail"
+    capture_state "failure-$test_name"
+    capture_failure_diagnostics
+    emit_junit
+
+    # Keep temp dirs for postmortem on failure.
+    E2E_CLEANUP_ITEMS=()
+    e2e_log "Preserving temp directory for debugging: $E2E_TEMP_DIR"
+    e2e_fail "$test_name failed: $detail"
+}
+
+run_case() {
+    local test_name="$1"
+    shift
+
+    local start_ms duration_ms
+    start_ms="$(timestamp_ms)"
+    if e2e_run "$@"; then
+        duration_ms=$(( $(timestamp_ms) - start_ms ))
+        record_test "$test_name" "pass" "$duration_ms"
+    else
+        duration_ms=$(( $(timestamp_ms) - start_ms ))
+        fail_suite "$test_name" "$duration_ms" "command failed (exit=${E2E_LAST_EXIT_CODE})"
+    fi
+}
+
+run_case_shell() {
+    local test_name="$1"
+    local shell_cmd="$2"
+    run_case "$test_name" bash -lc "$shell_cmd"
+}
+
+expect_case_failure() {
+    local test_name="$1"
+    shift
+    local start_ms duration_ms
+
+    start_ms="$(timestamp_ms)"
+    if e2e_run "$@"; then
+        duration_ms=$(( $(timestamp_ms) - start_ms ))
+        fail_suite "$test_name" "$duration_ms" "expected failure but command succeeded"
+    else
+        duration_ms=$(( $(timestamp_ms) - start_ms ))
+        record_test "$test_name" "pass" "$duration_ms" "expected failure observed (exit=${E2E_LAST_EXIT_CODE})"
+    fi
+}
+
+wait_for_mount_ready() {
+    local mount_point="$1"
+    local pid="$2"
+    local timeout_seconds="${3:-20}"
+    local elapsed=0
+
+    while ! mountpoint -q "$mount_point" 2>/dev/null; do
+        sleep 0.5
+        elapsed=$((elapsed + 1))
+
+        if ! kill -0 "$pid" 2>/dev/null; then
+            return 1
+        fi
+
+        if [[ $elapsed -ge $((timeout_seconds * 2)) ]]; then
+            return 2
+        fi
+    done
+    return 0
+}
+
+start_mount() {
+    local mode="$1"
+    local image="$2"
+    local mount_point="$3"
+    local timeout_seconds="${4:-20}"
+
+    if [[ "${SKIP_MOUNT:-0}" == "1" ]]; then
+        skip_suite "mount tests skipped (SKIP_MOUNT=1)"
+    fi
+    if [[ ! -e /dev/fuse ]]; then
+        skip_suite "/dev/fuse not available"
+    fi
+    if [[ ! -r /dev/fuse ]] || [[ ! -w /dev/fuse ]]; then
+        skip_suite "/dev/fuse not accessible"
+    fi
+
+    mkdir -p "$mount_point"
+    E2E_MOUNT_POINT="$mount_point"
+    CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT="$mount_point"
+    CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG="$E2E_LOG_DIR/mount_${mode}_$(basename "$mount_point").log"
+
+    local cmd=(cargo run -p ffs-cli --release -- mount "$image" "$mount_point")
+    if [[ "$mode" == "rw" ]]; then
+        cmd+=(--rw)
+    fi
+
+    e2e_log "Starting mount command: ${cmd[*]}"
+    e2e_log "Mount log: $CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG"
+    "${cmd[@]}" >"$CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG" 2>&1 &
+    CURRENT_MOUNT_PID=$!
+
+    local ready_result=0
+    if wait_for_mount_ready "$mount_point" "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" "$timeout_seconds"; then
+        ready_result=0
+    else
+        ready_result=$?
+    fi
+
+    if [[ $ready_result -eq 0 ]]; then
+        e2e_log "Mount ready at $mount_point (pid=$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID, mode=$mode)"
+        return 0
+    fi
+
+    local mount_rc=0
+    if kill -0 "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null; then
+        kill "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+        wait "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+        mount_rc=124
+    else
+        wait "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || mount_rc=$?
+    fi
+
+    e2e_log "Mount failed (mode=$mode rc=$mount_rc), tailing mount log:"
+    e2e_run tail -n 120 "$CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG" || true
+
+    if grep -qiE "allow_other only allowed if 'user_allow_other' is set" "$CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG"; then
+        skip_suite "FUSE present but user_allow_other is not enabled in /etc/fuse.conf"
+    fi
+    if [[ "$mode" == "rw" ]] && grep -qiE "btrfs read-write mount is not yet supported|read-write mount is not yet supported" "$CURRENT_MOUNT_LOG"; then
+        skip_suite "btrfs read-write mount is not yet supported in this build"
+    fi
+
+    if [[ $ready_result -eq 2 ]]; then
+        fail_suite "mount_${mode}" 0 "mount timed out after ${timeout_seconds}s"
+    fi
+    fail_suite "mount_${mode}" 0 "mount process exited before mount became ready"
+}
+
+stop_mount() {
+    local mount_point="${1:-$CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT}"
+
+    e2e_unmount "$mount_point"
+    if [[ -n "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" ]] && kill -0 "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null; then
+        kill "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+        sleep 0.5
+        if kill -0 "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null; then
+            kill -9 "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+        fi
+    fi
+    if [[ -n "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" ]]; then
+        wait "$CURRENT_MOUNT_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+    fi
+
+    CURRENT_MOUNT_PID=""
+    CURRENT_MOUNT_POINT=""
+}
+
+super_generation() {
+    local image="$1"
+    btrfs inspect-internal dump-super -f "$image" 2>/dev/null | awk '/^generation[[:space:]]/ { print $2; exit }'
+}
+
+super_root_bytenr() {
+    local image="$1"
+    btrfs inspect-internal dump-super -f "$image" 2>/dev/null | awk '/^root[[:space:]]/ { print $2; exit }'
+}
+
+prepare_btrfs_images() {
+    local base_image="$1"
+    local work_image="$2"
+    local fallback_fixture="$REPO_ROOT/tests/fixtures/images/btrfs_small.img"
+
+    if ! command -v mkfs.btrfs >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+        skip_suite "mkfs.btrfs not found"
+    fi
+    if ! command -v btrfs >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+        skip_suite "btrfs CLI not found"
+    fi
+
+    e2e_log "Preparing btrfs base image ($base_image, 256 MiB)."
+    run_case "image_allocate_256mb" dd if=/dev/zero of="$base_image" bs=1M count=256 status=none
+    run_case "image_mkfs_btrfs" mkfs.btrfs -f "$base_image"
+
+    if e2e_run cargo run -p ffs-cli --release -- inspect "$base_image" --json; then
+        e2e_log "Fresh mkfs.btrfs image is inspectable by current FrankenFS parser."
+    else
+        e2e_log "Fresh mkfs.btrfs image is not inspectable yet; falling back to fixture image."
+        if [[ ! -f "$fallback_fixture" ]]; then
+            fail_suite "image_fallback" 0 "fallback fixture missing: $fallback_fixture"
+        fi
+        run_case "image_fallback_copy" cp "$fallback_fixture" "$base_image"
+    fi
+
+    run_case "image_copy_work" cp "$base_image" "$work_image"
+}
+
+e2e_step "Phase 1: Build ffs-cli"
+run_case "build_ffs_cli" cargo build -p ffs-cli --release
+
+e2e_step "Phase 2: Prepare btrfs test image"
+BASE_IMAGE="$E2E_TEMP_DIR/base.btrfs"
+WORK_IMAGE="$E2E_TEMP_DIR/work.btrfs"
+prepare_btrfs_images "$BASE_IMAGE" "$WORK_IMAGE"
+run_case "inspect_work_image" cargo run -p ffs-cli --release -- inspect "$WORK_IMAGE" --json
+
+GEN_BEFORE="$(super_generation "$WORK_IMAGE")"
+ROOT_BEFORE="$(super_root_bytenr "$WORK_IMAGE")"
+e2e_log "Initial superblock generation=$GEN_BEFORE root_bytenr=$ROOT_BEFORE"
+
+e2e_step "Phase 3: Mount RW and execute btrfs write-path operations"
+MOUNT_RW="$E2E_TEMP_DIR/mnt_rw"
+start_mount rw "$WORK_IMAGE" "$MOUNT_RW" 20
+capture_state "rw-mounted-before-ops"
+
+run_case_shell "file_small_write" "printf 'small-line\\n' > '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt'"
+run_case_shell "file_small_verify" "grep -Fxq 'small-line' '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt'"
+
+run_case_shell "file_medium_write_4kb" "python3 -c \"from pathlib import Path; Path('$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin').write_bytes(b'M' * 4096)\""
+run_case_shell "file_medium_md5" "md5sum '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin' > '$E2E_LOG_DIR/medium.md5'"
+
+run_case_shell "file_large_write_1mb" "python3 -c \"from pathlib import Path; Path('$MOUNT_RW/large.bin').write_bytes(b'L' * (1024 * 1024))\""
+run_case_shell "file_large_md5" "md5sum '$MOUNT_RW/large.bin' > '$E2E_LOG_DIR/large.md5'"
+run_case_shell "file_overwrite_verify" "printf 'overwritten\\n' > '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt' && grep -Fxq 'overwritten' '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt'"
+run_case_shell "file_append" "printf 'append-line\\n' >> '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt' && grep -Fxq 'append-line' '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt'"
+
+run_case_shell "file_truncate_extend_2mb" "truncate -s 2097152 '$MOUNT_RW/large.bin'"
+run_case_shell "file_truncate_shrink_256b" "truncate -s 256 '$MOUNT_RW/large.bin' && test \"$(stat -c '%s' '$MOUNT_RW/large.bin')\" -eq 256"
+
+run_case "dir_mkdir_single" mkdir "$MOUNT_RW/single_dir"
+run_case "dir_mkdir_nested" mkdir -p "$MOUNT_RW/nested/a/b"
+run_case "dir_rmdir_empty" rmdir "$MOUNT_RW/single_dir"
+expect_case_failure "dir_rmdir_non_empty_fails" rmdir "$MOUNT_RW/nested"
+
+run_case_shell "name_rename_within_dir" "mv '$MOUNT_RW/small.txt' '$MOUNT_RW/small_renamed.txt'"
+run_case_shell "name_rename_across_dir" "mv '$MOUNT_RW/small_renamed.txt' '$MOUNT_RW/nested/a/small_renamed.txt'"
+run_case_shell "name_rename_over_existing" "printf 'target\\n' > '$MOUNT_RW/target.txt'; printf 'source\\n' > '$MOUNT_RW/source.txt'; mv -f '$MOUNT_RW/source.txt' '$MOUNT_RW/target.txt'; grep -Fxq 'source' '$MOUNT_RW/target.txt'"
+run_case "name_unlink_regular" rm "$MOUNT_RW/target.txt"
+
+run_case_shell "link_symlink_create" "ln -s '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin' '$MOUNT_RW/medium.link'"
+run_case_shell "link_symlink_readlink" "test \"$(readlink '$MOUNT_RW/medium.link')\" = '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin'"
+run_case "link_symlink_unlink" rm "$MOUNT_RW/medium.link"
+
+run_case_shell "link_hardlink_create" "ln '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin' '$MOUNT_RW/medium.hard'"
+run_case_shell "link_hardlink_inode_match" "test \"$(stat -c '%i' '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin')\" = \"$(stat -c '%i' '$MOUNT_RW/medium.hard')\""
+run_case_shell "link_hardlink_shared_data" "printf 'X' >> '$MOUNT_RW/medium.hard'; tail -c 1 '$MOUNT_RW/medium.bin' | grep -Fxq 'X'"
+run_case "link_hardlink_unlink" rm "$MOUNT_RW/medium.hard"
+
+e2e_step "Phase 3.1: COW generation checks"
+run_case_shell "cow_hot_file_seed" "printf 'cow-seed\\n' > '$MOUNT_RW/cow_hot.txt'"
+for i in $(seq 1 10); do
+    run_case_shell "cow_rewrite_$i" "printf 'rewrite-%02d\\n' $i > '$MOUNT_RW/cow_hot.txt'"
+done
+run_case_shell "cow_fsync_hot_file" "python3 -c \"import os; path='$MOUNT_RW/cow_hot.txt'; fd=os.open(path, os.O_RDWR); os.fsync(fd); os.close(fd)\""
+run_case "cow_sync" sync
+
+capture_state "rw-mounted-after-ops"
+
+e2e_step "Phase 3.2: Clean unmount"
+run_case "rw_stop_mount" stop_mount "$MOUNT_RW"
+if mountpoint -q "$MOUNT_RW" 2>/dev/null; then
+    fail_suite "rw_unmount_verify" 0 "RW mount point still mounted: $MOUNT_RW"
+fi
+
+GEN_AFTER="$(super_generation "$WORK_IMAGE")"
+ROOT_AFTER="$(super_root_bytenr "$WORK_IMAGE")"
+e2e_log "Post-write superblock generation=$GEN_AFTER root_bytenr=$ROOT_AFTER"
+
+if [[ -n "$GEN_BEFORE" ]] && [[ -n "$GEN_AFTER" ]]; then
+    if (( GEN_AFTER < GEN_BEFORE )); then
+        fail_suite "cow_generation_monotonic" 0 "generation regressed: before=$GEN_BEFORE after=$GEN_AFTER"
+    fi
+    record_test "cow_generation_monotonic" "pass" 0 "generation before=$GEN_BEFORE after=$GEN_AFTER"
+fi
+if [[ -n "$ROOT_BEFORE" ]] && [[ -n "$ROOT_AFTER" ]]; then
+    record_test "cow_root_recorded" "pass" 0 "root before=$ROOT_BEFORE after=$ROOT_AFTER"
+fi
+
+e2e_step "Phase 4: Remount RO and verify persistence"
+MOUNT_RO="$E2E_TEMP_DIR/mnt_ro"
+start_mount ro "$WORK_IMAGE" "$MOUNT_RO" 20
+
+run_case_shell "persist_small_data" "grep -Fxq 'append-line' '$MOUNT_RO/nested/a/small_renamed.txt'"
+run_case_shell "persist_medium_size" "test \"$(stat -c '%s' '$MOUNT_RO/medium.bin')\" -ge 4096"
+run_case_shell "persist_large_size" "test \"$(stat -c '%s' '$MOUNT_RO/large.bin')\" -eq 256"
+run_case_shell "persist_mode_preserved" "stat -c '%a' '$MOUNT_RO/nested/a/small_renamed.txt' >/dev/null"
+run_case_shell "persist_mtime_present" "test \"$(stat -c '%Y' '$MOUNT_RO/nested/a/small_renamed.txt')\" -gt 0"
+
+capture_state "ro-mounted-verification"
+run_case "ro_stop_mount" stop_mount "$MOUNT_RO"
+if mountpoint -q "$MOUNT_RO" 2>/dev/null; then
+    fail_suite "ro_unmount_verify" 0 "RO mount point still mounted: $MOUNT_RO"
+fi
+
+e2e_log "Cleanup is managed by scripts/e2e/lib.sh trap (EXIT)."
+emit_junit
+e2e_pass

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+# ffs_repair_5pct_e2e.sh - Deterministic 5% corruption auto-repair E2E
+set -euo pipefail
+
+REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/../.." && pwd)"
+cd "$REPO_ROOT"
+
+source "$REPO_ROOT/scripts/e2e/lib.sh"
+
+e2e_init "ffs_repair_5pct_e2e"
+e2e_print_env
+
+e2e_step "Run 5% corruption auto-repair scenario"
+ARTIFACT_DIR="$E2E_LOG_DIR/repair_5pct"
+mkdir -p "$ARTIFACT_DIR"
+
+# Keep this runner isolated from other in-flight cargo jobs.
+export CARGO_TARGET_DIR="${CARGO_TARGET_DIR:-target-codex-ruby}"
+export FFS_REPAIR_E2E_ARTIFACT_DIR="$ARTIFACT_DIR"
+
+e2e_assert cargo test -p ffs-repair e2e_survive_five_percent_random_block_corruption_with_daemon -- --nocapture
+
+e2e_step "Validate generated artifacts"
+e2e_assert_file "$ARTIFACT_DIR/before_checksums.txt"
+e2e_assert_file "$ARTIFACT_DIR/after_checksums.txt"
+e2e_assert_file "$ARTIFACT_DIR/corruption_plan.json"
+e2e_assert_file "$ARTIFACT_DIR/recovery_evidence.jsonl"
+
+# End-to-end guarantee: recovered content must exactly match baseline.
+e2e_assert cmp "$ARTIFACT_DIR/before_checksums.txt" "$ARTIFACT_DIR/after_checksums.txt"
+
+if command -v python3 >/dev/null 2>&1; then
+    e2e_assert python3 -c "import json, pathlib; p = pathlib.Path('$ARTIFACT_DIR/corruption_plan.json'); d = json.loads(p.read_text()); assert d['corruption_percent'] == 5; assert d['total_corrupted_blocks'] > 0"
+fi
+
+e2e_run wc -l "$ARTIFACT_DIR/recovery_evidence.jsonl"
+
+e2e_pass
